preFix.title: Using cardboard as a carbon source in compost
preFix.subtitle: Which cardboard works, how to prepare it, and how much to add
preFix.excerpt: Cardboard is one of the best readily available browns for a home compost heap or wormery. Corrugated card has a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of around 500:1 and breaks down steadily when shredded or torn. Understanding which types to use, how to prepare them, and how much the heap needs relative to its greens prevents the two most common cardboard composting mistakes: using too-thick layers and adding coated or printed card that resists breakdown.
preFix.sourceNotes: Kim Woodward, Composting for Sustainable Development (2018). RHS gardening advice on composting cardboard 2024. WRAP Household Compostable Materials guide.

content.text: Most home compost heaps run short of
browns

, the high-carbon dry materials that balance the nitrogen-rich kitchen and garden waste. Cardboard is the most accessible source of browns for the average UK household, which generates several square metres of delivery packaging per month. With a
C:N ratio
of around 400 to 500:1, corrugated card is a very high-carbon source. A single large parcel box, shredded, provides enough carbon to balance 1 to 2 kg of fresh kitchen waste.
Types that work well
Plain corrugated cardboard, the best choice. The fluted inner layer increases surface area and holds moisture, speeding breakdown.
Cereal boxes and food-grade card, fine in small quantities. Remove the plastic inner bag or lining.
Egg boxes, break down readily and improve heap aeration as they decompose.
Kitchen roll tubes and toilet roll tubes, fine.
Types to exclude
Wax-coated card and heavily printed card (some pizza boxes, glossy outer packaging): wax and ink slow rot and may leave tiny plastic pieces in the compost.
Plastic-laminated card (Tetrapak cartons, frozen-food boxes): the plastic film does not rot down. It leaves bits of plastic in the compost.
Staples and metallic tape, remove before composting. They will not harm the heap but will end up in the finished compost.
Preparation

Tear or shred the card into pieces no larger than approximately 20 × 20 cm. Smaller pieces break down faster. Large intact sheets create dense mats that block air and water movement, the most common cardboard composting mistake.

Wet the card before adding to the heap. Dry corrugated card in a layered heap takes months to begin breaking down; wet card starts within days. Soak a stack of torn pieces briefly in a bucket of water, drain slightly, then add.

Mix with greens rather than layering. A thick layer of card above a thick layer of kitchen waste creates two separate environments. Mix the wet card through the fresh addition, or alternate thin layers (50 to 100 mm) of card with thin layers of kitchen waste.
How much to use

As a rough guide, add a roughly equal volume of torn, wet cardboard for every volume of kitchen waste. Because corrugated card compresses as it breaks down, the starting volume ratio will produce a balanced heap by the time the material is mid-decomposition. If the heap is smelling or wet and slimy (too much nitrogen), double the card ratio. If the heap is dry and not heating (too much carbon), reduce the card and add more fresh materials.
